From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian state flag

New South Wales

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |
| Flag of New South Wales | |
| [Use](/wiki/Vexillological_symbol "Vexillological symbol") | State flag [Small vexillological symbol or pictogram in black and white showing the different uses of the flag](/wiki/File:FIAV_110000.svg "Civil and state flag") [Small vexillological symbol or pictogram in black and white showing the different uses of the flag](/wiki/File:FIAV_normal.svg "Normal or de jure version of flag, or obverse side") [Reverse side is mirror image of obverse side](/wiki/File:FIAV_mirror.svg "Reverse side is mirror image of obverse side") |
| Proportion | 1:2 |
| Adopted | 1876; 150 years ago (1876) |
| Design | A British [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign") defaced with the state badge in the fly |
|  | |

The current state flag of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") was officially adopted in 1876. The flag is based on the [defaced](/wiki/Defacement_(flag) "Defacement (flag)") British [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign") with the state badge located in the [fly](/wiki/Flag_terminology#Description_of_standard_flag_parts_and_terms "Flag terminology"). The badge, based on the [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_South_Wales "Coat of arms of New South Wales"), is a white disc with the [cross of St George](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross"), a golden lion passant guardant in the centre of the cross and an eight-pointed gold star on each arm of the cross.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Badge_of_New_South_Wales.svg/250px-Badge_of_New_South_Wales.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Badge_of_New_South_Wales.svg)

[Badge of New South Wales](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_South_Wales "Coat of arms of New South Wales")

## Symbolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Symbolism")]

As a variant of the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign"), the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") is displayed in the canton. The [St George's Cross](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross"), the [Southern Cross](/wiki/Southern_Cross "Southern Cross") and the 'Lion in the South' are displayed in the [Badge of New South Wales](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_South_Wales "Coat of arms of New South Wales") on the flag. The St George's Cross is used on the ensign of the British [Royal Navy](/wiki/British_Naval_ensigns "British Naval ensigns"), with a golden, 8-pointed star placed on each arm of the cross. This symbolises the maritime origins of New South Wales, with seafarers relying upon the Southern Cross to navigate the seas, and the role of the navy in protecting the State.[[1]](#cite_note-gullick-1)

The 'Lion in the South' is taken from the three golden lions on a red field on the [arms of England](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_England "Coat of arms of England"), and symbolises both the sovereignty of New South Wales and the offspring of an old country. It represent the origins of the founders of the [Colony](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales") of New South Wales as well as the independence of their succeeding generations.[[2]](#cite_note-gullick2-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The first flag of New South Wales was adopted in 1867. It too was a defaced British Blue Ensign with the letters "NSW" in white located in the fly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The flag was a response to the passing of the British [Colonial Naval Defence Act 1865](/wiki/Colonial_Naval_Defence_Act_1865 "Colonial Naval Defence Act 1865") which allowed any British colony its own warships and variant of the [Blue Ensign](/wiki/Blue_Ensign "Blue Ensign").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

New South Wales then adopted a second flag in 1870 and it was almost identical to that of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") (with gold stars; 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 points). This flag was also a defaced British Blue Ensign with the "Governor's Badge" located in the fly. The badge was the [Southern Cross](/wiki/Crux "Crux") and an imperial crown situated above the Southern Cross. The difference between this flag and that of the [Victorian flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Victoria_(Australia) "Flag of Victoria (Australia)") was that the stars were gold and ranged from five to nine points with each star having one point pointing to the bottom of the flag.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The present flag was adopted due to criticisms from the [British Admiralty](/wiki/British_Admiralty "British Admiralty") that the previous design was too similar to the design of the [Victorian flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Victoria_(Australia) "Flag of Victoria (Australia)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The state badge was designed by the Colonial Architect [James Barnet](/wiki/James_Barnet "James Barnet") and Captain [Francis Hixson](/wiki/Francis_Hixson "Francis Hixson"), a retired Royal Navy officer.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

### Redesign proposal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Redesign proposal")]

In the mid-1990s, a series of proposals to redesign the New South Wales state flag emerged, gaining significant public attention in the lead-up to the 2000 Sydney Olympics. The initiative, originally promoted by the *Sydney Morning Herald* in November 1996, aimed to address the perceived disconnect between the existing flag and the state's identity. The *Herald* argued that the flag designed served "a British rather than a NSW purpose" and failed to resonate with modern New South Wales' cultural identity and heritage.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

In November 1996, two alternative flag designs were unveiled and displayed prominently to gauge public interest. One was inspired by the design of the Canadian flag, whilst the other included a Southern Cross. Both designs incorporated the Waratah.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

Advocates of the redesign included former premiers, political leaders from both the Liberal and Labor parties, the Lord Mayor of Sydney, and notable sports figures. Supporters argued that a new flag would better reflect the state's evolving cultural and social identity, particularly as Sydney prepared to host the Olympics—a global showcase of the state's heritage.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

However, the proposals also faced criticism. Traditionalists expressed concern that altering the flag might undermine historical continuity, while others feared it signaled an imminent move towards republicanism.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) A 1996 *Herald AGB-McNair* poll revealed that only 12% of New South Wales residents could accurately describe the existing flag, highlighting a potential lack of public attachment to its symbols.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

In response to the debate, proponents suggested introducing legislation to authorize a public referendum on the flag redesign, potentially aligning the vote with the 1999 state election. This timeline would allow for extensive public discussion before a new flag could be adopted ahead of the 2000 Olympics.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) Despite the momentum, the proposal ultimately did not progress beyond the discussion phase.

## Other flags

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other flags")]
